file_name: Seeja and Geetha - 2023 - Effective melanoma classification using inter neighbour mean order interleaved pattern on dermoscopy.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses AI/ML methods for melanoma classification on dermoscopy images. Specifically, it employs a U-Net algorithm (based on Convolutional Neural Networks) for skin lesion segmentation, extracts features using the novel Inter Neighbour Mean Order Interleaved Pattern (INMOIP), and classifies lesions using machine learning classifiers (Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, K-Nearest Neighbor) and a deep learning classifier (VGG-16).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a novel feature descriptor (INMOIP), segmentation using U-Net, and classification using multiple ML/DL models (RF, SVM, KNN, VGG-16). It reports specific quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F-score) on three datasets (ISBI-2016, ISBI-2017, ISBI-2019) and compares them against existing methods, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Effective melanoma classification using inter neighbour mean order interleaved pattern on dermoscopy images
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated melanoma classification system using dermoscopy images to address the challenges of early diagnosis. The methodology involves segmenting skin lesions using a U-Net algorithm and extracting robust texture features via a novel Inter Neighbour Mean Order Interleaved Pattern (INMOIP) descriptor. These features are then classified using Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, K-Nearest Neighbor, and VGG-16 models. The approach was evaluated on the ISBI-2016, ISBI-2017, and ISBI-2019 datasets using accuracy, precision, recall, and F-score metrics. The VGG-16 classifier achieved the highest classification accuracies of 94.87%, 86.88%, and 93.65% on the respective datasets. The results demonstrate that the proposed INMOIP method outperforms existing approaches in melanoma classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target class for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Benign/Non-melanoma) across ISBI-2016, ISBI-2017, and ISBI-2019 datasets.

Grouped as the negative class (Non-Melanoma) for binary classification. In ISBI-2017, this group specifically included benign nevi and seborrheic keratosis. In ISBI-2019, it included melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, and dermatofibroma.
Mentioned as one of the three classes in the ISBI-2017 dataset, but grouped into the 'benign' (non-melanoma) category for the binary classification task in this study.

Listed as one of the non-melanoma lesion types in the ISBI-2019 dataset, but grouped into the 'benign' (non-melanoma) category for the binary classification task.

Listed as one of the non-melanoma lesion types in the ISBI-2019 dataset, but grouped into the 'benign' (non-melanoma) category for the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'Melanoma' (malignant) or 'Benign' (non-melanoma) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the purpose is to classify skin lesions into 'benign or melanoma' classes. It uses Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, K-Nearest Neighbor, and VGG-16 classifiers to predict discrete diagnostic labels (Melanoma vs. Benign/Non-melanoma) on the ISBI-2016, ISBI-2017, and ISBI-2019 datasets. Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, and F-score for these discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) datasets, specifically the ISBI 2016 challenge dataset containing dermoscopy images of skin lesions. It includes ground truth for segmentation.

use.training: Used for segmentation training (90% of Part 1, i.e., 810 images) and classification training (Part 3, 900 images) via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for segmentation testing (10% of Part 1, i.e., 90 images) and via 10-fold cross-validation for classification.
use.testing: Used for classification testing (Part 3, 900 images) via 10-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017
datasets_used.description: Part of the ISIC datasets, containing skin lesion pictures with segmentation masks, superpixel masks, and annotations. It was used exclusively for testing in this study.

use.testing: Used exclusively for testing the classification model trained on ISBI 2016.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2019
datasets_used.description: Part of the ISIC datasets, containing nine distinct types of skin lesions. A subset of 3000 samples was selected for this study to prevent hardware difficulties.
classes_used: Non-melanoma (including Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Dermatofibroma, etc.)

use.training: Used for classification training via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for classification validation via 10-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for classification testing via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the classification performance on the ISBI-2016 and ISBI-2019 datasets for training and testing data.
Used to evaluate the quantitative performance of the U-Net segmentation algorithm.
Used as performance measures to evaluate the quantitative results of the classification models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between classifiers were made using absolute differences in accuracy percentages (e.g., "+ 0.8% of accuracy than RF classifier") without statistical significance testing.

ISBI-2016.notes: INMOIP with VGG-16 achieved +0.8% accuracy over RF, +4.13% over SVM, and +10.56% over KNN. RF classifier achieved higher precision than VGG-16.

ISBI-2017.notes: VGG-16 improved accuracy by +0.34% over RF, +5.15% over SVM, and +9.96% over KNN.

ISBI-2019.notes: VGG-16 achieved best performance in accuracy, recall, and F-score. SVM achieved slightly higher precision than VGG-16.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (used as a classifier with extracted INMOIP features; standard VGG-16 architecture described)

architecture_details.layers: 16 convolutional layers, 5 max-pooling layers (2x2 window size), 3 fully connected layers
architecture_details.blocks: 5 convolutional blocks: Block 1 (2 conv layers, 1 max-pool), Block 2 (2 conv layers, 1 max-pool), Block 3 (3 conv layers, 1 max-pool), Block 4 (3 conv layers, 1 max-pool), Block 5 (3 conv layers, 1 max-pool)

training_details.hardware: Core i7, 9th Gen processor with NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1650
training_details.training_time: Approximately 30 hours (mentioned in comparison context, not explicitly for this specific run's total duration, but computational time per sample is 2.92s)

classification_or_output_head.details: Three fully connected layers following the convolutional blocks. Used for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Benign).

preprocessing_techniques: Skin lesion segmentation using U-Net algorithm (CNN-based) to isolate the lesion region from the background.
Feature extraction using Inter Neighbour Mean Order Interleaved Pattern (INMOIP) descriptor on segmented images.
Histogram generation for the extracted features.
conclusions: The proposed automated melanoma classification system, utilizing the novel Inter Neighbour Mean Order Interleaved Pattern (INMOIP) feature descriptor combined with the VGG-16 classifier, demonstrates exceptional performance and outperforms existing approaches on the ISBI-2016, ISBI-2017, and ISBI-2019 datasets.
On the ISBI-2016 dataset, the INMOIP with VGG-16 classifier achieved the highest classification accuracy of 94.87%, surpassing Random Forest (94.07%), SVM (90.74%), and KNN (84.31%).
On the ISBI-2017 dataset, the INMOIP with VGG-16 classifier achieved an accuracy of 86.88%, improving upon Random Forest (86.54%), SVM (81.73%), and KNN (76.92%).
On the ISBI-2019 dataset, the INMOIP with VGG-16 classifier achieved an accuracy of 93.65%, outperforming Random Forest (92.79%), SVM (88.11%), and KNN (82.56%).
The U-Net segmentation algorithm effectively isolated skin lesion regions, achieving a Dice-coefficiency of 88.02%, which facilitated precise feature extraction from the lesion areas.

The INMOIP feature descriptor enhances classification accuracy by incorporating inter-channel texture information and interleaved patterns from multiple color spaces (R, G, B), addressing limitations of deep learning models that may not measure local differences between neighbors effectively.

While VGG-16 generally provided the best overall performance, the Random Forest classifier achieved higher precision than VGG-16 on the ISBI-2016 dataset, and SVM achieved slightly higher precision than VGG-16 on the ISBI-2019 dataset.

The proposed method is effective for binary classification of melanoma versus benign/non-melanoma lesions, aiding in early detection and potentially lowering death rates by providing accurate diagnosis support for dermatologists.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; the model trained on ISBI-2016 was tested on ISBI-2017, but no independent external dataset was used to verify generalizability.

The ISBI-2019 dataset was artificially balanced (1500 melanoma, 1500 non-melanoma) by selecting 3000 samples to prevent hardware difficulties, which does not reflect the natural class imbalance found in clinical populations.
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between classifiers, relying solely on absolute accuracy differences.
Hyperparameters for the VGG-16 classifier (optimizer, loss function, learning rate) are not reported, limiting reproducibility.
The U-Net segmentation Dice coefficient (88.02%) is reported only for the ISBI-2016 dataset; performance on ISBI-2017 and ISBI-2019 segmentation is not provided.

The study uses a hybrid approach where handcrafted features (INMOIP) are fed into a deep learning classifier (VGG-16), which is an unconventional architecture compared to end-to-end deep learning models.
The authors acknowledge that the proposed method can assist dermatologists but do not provide clinical workflow integration or user study results.
